1.synthesis: As the Jerusalem church grows, a complaint arises among the Hellenist Jewish believers that their widows are being overlooked in the daily distribution of food, while the Hebrew widows are not (Acts 6:1). The Twelve recognize they cannot neglect the word of God to wait tables, so they call the community to choose seven men 'of good repute, full of the Spirit and of wisdom' to oversee the distribution (Acts 6:2–3). The Seven are chosen—all with Greek names, suggesting Hellenist background—and appointed by prayer and the laying on of hands (Acts 6:5–6). The word of God continues to increase; the number of disciples multiplies greatly in Jerusalem, and a large number of priests become obedient to the faith (Acts 6:7).
1.historical_context: The distinction between 'Hellenists' and 'Hebrews' in the Jerusalem community reflects the cultural divide between diaspora Jews who spoke Greek and read the LXX and Aramaic-speaking Palestinian Jews. Widows in the ancient world were among the most economically vulnerable, and communal care for them was a central obligation of Jewish piety (Deuteronomy 14:29; Isaiah 1:17). The seven names—Stephen, Philip, Prochorus, Nicanor, Timon, Parmenas, and Nicolaus—are all Greek, suggesting the community chose men from the aggrieved group to oversee distribution. The laying on of hands is a commissioning gesture rooted in Moses' ordination of Joshua (Numbers 27:18) and will become the standard mark of apostolic appointment in Acts.
1.literary_structure: The passage follows Acts' pattern of community problem → Spirit-led solution → growth summary. The Twelve's refusal to neglect the word for tables is not a demotion of service ministry but a recognition that the community needs differentiated leadership. The growth summary (v.7) is one of Luke's six progress summaries in Acts (cf. 2:47; 9:31; 12:24; 16:5; 19:20) and marks the end of a narrative unit. The priests 'obedient to the faith' (v.7) represents a remarkable reversal of the priestly establishment that opposed the apostles in chapters 4–5.
1.theological_application: The Seven's appointment is the church's first institutional response to communal injustice—the structural inequity of the food distribution is addressed by creating new leadership, not by moralizing. This models the church's ongoing vocation: when systemic inequity emerges, it requires structural response, not only individual charity. The principle that word-ministry and service-ministry are both essential, and that neither can simply absorb the other, remains foundational for church organization. The community's role in choosing the Seven (v.3, 'pick out from among you') demonstrates that apostolic authority and congregational discernment are meant to work together.
1.christology: The Seven are chosen to serve in Jesus' name, representing his ongoing provision for the community through its members. Stephen is described as 'full of faith and of the Holy Spirit' (Acts 6:5)—the same Spirit who came at Pentecost and who continues the risen Christ's presence in the community. The growth of the word (v.7) is the growth of the kingdom Jesus proclaimed and inaugurated: his ministry of proclamation and service continues through the body he has formed, differentiated but unified in Spirit-filled witness.

8.synthesis: Stephen, full of grace and power, performs great wonders and signs among the people (Acts 6:8). Opposition arises from members of a synagogue of diaspora Jews; they cannot withstand his wisdom and the Spirit with which he speaks (Acts 6:9–10). They secretly instigate false witnesses: Stephen speaks against Moses, against God, against the temple, and against the customs (Acts 6:11–14). Brought before the council, all who sat there saw his face 'like the face of an angel' (Acts 6:15). The charges anticipate the speech of chapter 7—the council has accused Stephen of the very insights that Luke will present as prophetically correct.
8.historical_context: The 'Synagogue of the Freedmen' (v.9, Libertinoi) likely consisted of Jews whose ancestors had been taken as slaves to Rome and subsequently freed—Cyrenians, Alexandrians, and those from Cilicia and Asia. The charge of speaking against 'the holy place and the law' (Acts 6:13) mirrors the charge against Jesus at his trial (Mark 14:57–58)—Luke is deliberately paralleling Stephen's passion with Jesus'. The accusation about Jesus changing 'the customs that Moses delivered' presupposes a Hellenistic Jewish community acutely sensitive to temple and Torah as boundary markers of identity. Stephen's angelic appearance (v.15) echoes Moses on Sinai (Exodus 34:29–30) and is Luke's signal that the Spirit of God is present in the accused.
8.literary_structure: The passage is the setup for Stephen's speech (ch.7) and martyrdom (7:54–60). The charges function as rhetorical prompts that the speech will address and subvert: Stephen is accused of speaking against Moses, the temple, and the law; his address will demonstrate that Israel's leadership has consistently rejected the prophets and the one who fulfilled them. Luke's parallel between Stephen's trial and Jesus' trial—false witnesses, the Sanhedrin, the face shining—frames Stephen as the first Christian martyr in the pattern of the Christ.
8.theological_application: The pattern of Spirit-empowered witness drawing institutional opposition will repeat throughout Acts. The charges against Stephen—that he undermines the temple and the law—will eventually be the charges against Paul as well. The church must expect that prophetic witness to the gospel's transforming claim on religious and social institutions will produce conflict. Stephen's angelic face (v.15) is Luke's theological commentary: the one who is being falsely accused is vindicated by divine presence even before speaking a word. The church's witness in the face of hostile authority is sustained by the same Spirit.
8.christology: Stephen's wonders and signs (v.8) are performed in the name of Jesus—the continuation of Jesus' own ministry through the Spirit-filled community. His wisdom that his opponents cannot answer (Acts 6:10) echoes Jesus' promise: 'I will give you words and wisdom that none of your adversaries will be able to withstand or contradict' (Luke 21:15). Stephen is about to show that the charges of undermining Moses and the temple are false—the one truly faithful to Moses and the prophets is the one who recognizes their fulfillment in Jesus.
